notes: The general instruction regarding offerings is followed by the specific requirement for a blemish-free male animal.
The requirement concerning the acceptable animal is expanded with instructions about voluntary presentation before the LORD.
The worshiper presenting the offering is next instructed to lay hands upon the sacrifice.
Identification with the sacrifice through laying on of hands precedes the sacrificial slaughter and blood ritual.
After the blood ritual, the sacrifice is skinned and divided according to the ritual order.
Preparation of the sacrifice is followed by preparation of the altar fire by the priests.
The spotless sacrificial animal foreshadows Christ offering Himself without blemish.
The transfer symbolism in laying hands on the sacrifice parallels the LORD laying iniquity upon the suffering servant.
The blood sacrifice principle continues into New Testament theology regarding remission of sins.
After preparing the altar fire, the priests arrange the sacrifice upon the altar.
The arranged sacrifice is washed and then fully burned before the LORD.
The flock offering ritual repeats the washing and burning procedures established for herd offerings.
Both passages require a spotless male animal for burnt offerings.
The acceptable flock offering is followed by instructions for slaughter and blood application.
After the blood ritual, the sacrifice is divided and arranged upon the altar.
The prepared sacrifice is washed and completely consumed on the altar.
The instructions for acceptable bird offerings are followed by priestly preparation procedures.
After the bird is presented and blood applied, unwanted portions are removed.
The bird offering preparation concludes with complete burning upon the altar.
The spotless sacrificial animal foreshadows Christ as a lamb without blemish and without spot.
The pleasing aroma of the burnt offering anticipates Christ's sacrificial offering to God.
The allowance for bird offerings continues into the temple practices observed during the time of Jesus.
The preparation of the grain offering is followed by priestly presentation and burning of the memorial portion.
After the memorial portion is burned, the remaining offering is assigned to the priests.
Both passages describe unleavened grain offerings prepared in different cooking methods.
The pan-cooked offering instructions are followed by commands to divide it and pour oil upon it.
Both instructions concern grain offerings prepared with flour and oil using different vessels.
The grain offering is presented to the priest, who then burns the memorial portion upon the altar.
Following the altar memorial, the remaining portion becomes the priests' provision.
The unleavened offering symbolizes purity and foreshadows the New Testament imagery of sincerity and truth without leaven.
The pleasing sacrificial offering imagery continues in Paul's description of spiritual gifts offered unto God.
The sweet savour offering anticipates Christ's sacrificial offering unto God.
After instructions concerning priestly portions, regulations regarding prohibited ingredients are introduced.
The prohibition of leaven clarifies and reinforces the unleavened requirement in baked grain offerings.
The exclusion of leaven foreshadows New Testament imagery of purity and separation from corruption.
The instruction regarding firstfruit offerings is expanded with details about acceptable preparation.
The covenant symbolism of salt continues in later covenant language concerning permanence and faithfulness.
The symbolic significance of salt reappears in Jesus' teaching about covenant faithfulness and preservation.
The dedication of firstfruits unto the LORD continues as a principle of honoring God with increase.
Firstfruit imagery later becomes symbolic of believers dedicated unto God.
The use of oil and frankincense repeats the foundational grain offering formula established earlier in the chapter.
After the offering is prepared with oil and frankincense, the priest burns the memorial portion.
The requirements for an acceptable peace offering are followed by instructions for laying hands and blood application.
After the offering is slaughtered and blood applied, the fat portions are dedicated upon the altar.
The instructions for herd peace offerings are expanded to include flock offerings.
The general instructions regarding flock offerings are followed by specific instructions concerning lamb offerings.
The lamb offering instructions are expanded further to include goat offerings.
Because the fat and blood belong unto the LORD in sacrifice, Israel is forbidden from consuming them.
The requirement for offerings without blemish is repeated later in Levitical sacrificial law.
The sacrificial system foreshadows sanctification through the offering of Christ.
The offering devoted unto God is later reframed spiritually as believers presenting themselves as living sacrifices.
The apostolic instructions in Acts continue the prohibition regarding blood consumption.
General instructions for sin offerings are followed by priest-specific sin requirements.
Priestly sin condition is followed by requirement to bring a bull to the tabernacle.
The offering is brought before the LORD and then blood is taken for atonement ritual.
Blood is brought into the tabernacle and then sprinkled seven times before the LORD.
Sprinkling blood before the LORD is followed by application to altar horns.
After blood application, fat portions are removed for offering.
Removed portions are identified and prepared for burning.
Sin offering fat removal follows similar pattern to peace offerings.
General sin offering instructions expand into detailed blood ritual procedures.
Sin offering contrasts with voluntary peace offerings in Levitical system.
Blood sprinkling foreshadows Christ's cleansing of conscience through sacrifice.
Altar purification points toward believer access through Christ's blood.
Burnt offering is a pleasing aroma; sin offering is taken outside the camp indicating judgment and impurity removal.
The sin offering for priests transitions into instructions for corporate sin.
Unintentional corporate sin is followed by required atoning sacrifice.
The prescribed sacrifice is followed by laying on of hands and slaughter.
After slaughter, blood is brought into the tabernacle by the priest.
Blood is brought into the tabernacle and then sprinkled before the LORD as atonement.
Blood sprinkling reflects earlier atonement principles in Leviticus sin offering structure.
Laying hands and sacrificial death foreshadow New Covenant cleansing through Christ.
After the communal sin offering procedure, the text transitions to sin offerings for rulers.
General ruler sin condition is expanded into specific sacrificial requirement.
The prescribed offering is followed by laying hands and slaughter.
After slaughter, priest performs blood application ritual.
Blood ritual is followed by burning fat and declaration of forgiveness.
Leader and congregation sin offerings share identical blood application structure.
Forgiveness formula repeats across different sin offering categories.
General sin condition is followed by required offering provision.
Identification with sin offering precedes its slaughter.
After slaughter, blood ritual is performed by the priest.
Blood manipulation is followed by burning of fat portions.
Ritual structure transitions from herd/goat offering to lamb option.
Lamb offering is followed by slaughter and blood application procedures.
After slaughter, priests perform the same blood ritual as earlier offerings.
Completion of sacrificial ritual results in atonement and forgiveness.
Goat offering instructions are expanded by lamb offering alternative.
Blood application procedures are repeated in both goat and lamb offerings.
Atonement through sacrifice connects back to foundational Levitical burnt offering system.
Witness withholding testimony leads into broader purity violations.
Uncleanness and oath violations both produce guilt under Mosaic law.
Realization of guilt leads directly to required confession.
Confession is followed by required trespass offering.
The sacrificial requirement is expanded to accommodate economic inability.
If the person cannot afford birds, the offering is further reduced to flour.
Confession of sin parallels David’s confession against God alone.
Sacrificial atonement system points toward final forgiveness in Christ.
Provision for the poor in atonement reflects New Testament grace principles.
The sin offering is presented and followed by priestly atonement and forgiveness.
Forgiveness framework transitions into instructions for trespass offerings.
Conditional definition of trespass is followed by required sacrificial response.
The ram offering requirement is expanded with restitution obligations.
Restitution law is grounded in the principle that even unintentional sin creates guilt.
Recognition of guilt is followed by sacrificial atonement through a ram.
The trespass offering process concludes with confirmation of guilt against the LORD.
Blood sacrifice requirement anticipates New Testament theology of atonement through sacrifice.
Restitution principle is reflected in Zacchaeus' repentance and repayment.
General sin condition is followed by requirement to restore what was taken.
Restitution requirement is expanded into cases of violent theft.
Restoration is intensified by requiring an additional fifth as penalty.
After restitution, the trespass offering is required before the LORD.
The priest completes atonement after the trespass offering is brought.
Restoration principle repeated with emphasis on different forms of theft.
Principle of giving more than what was taken echoes Jesus' teaching on generosity and restitution.
Introduction is followed by specific instructions for the burnt offering law.
Instruction for burnt offering is followed by its continual burning requirement.
Continual burning requirement expands into priestly handling of altar fire and garments.
The command to keep fire burning is intensified into absolute permanence.
Burnt offering law concludes and transitions into grain offering law.
Introduction of grain offering law followed by instruction on burning memorial portion.
After memorial portion is burned, remaining portion is eaten by priests.
Priestly consumption is restricted by purity rules excluding leaven.
Purity restriction expands into statute governing priestly participation.
Statute transitions into new priestly offering instructions at consecration.
Daily grain offering is followed by specific preparation method.
Preparation is followed by priestly offering duty across generations.
Priestly grain offering is distinguished by being wholly burnt and not eaten.
Transition from grain offering laws to sin offering regulations.
General introduction leads into specific instructions about sin offering location.
Sacrificial location is followed by priestly consumption rules.
Consumption rules are followed by purity handling regulations.
Purity handling extends to vessel contamination rules.
Object purification rules lead to priestly consumption restriction.
Priestly eating is restricted when blood is brought into the tabernacle.
Both verses emphasize boundaries of consumption tied to holiness and purity.
Introduction of trespass offering law is followed by its sacrificial procedure.
Blood ritual is followed by offering of fat portions.
Fat offering is followed by removal of internal organs.
Removal of organs precedes burning of the sacrifice on the altar.
Trespass offering shares holiness and burning themes with burnt offering system.
Burning of sacrificial offering foreshadows once-for-all sacrifice of Christ.
Instructions for priestly consumption are followed by clarification of legal equivalence between offerings.
The legal equivalence of offerings expands into specific priestly entitlements.
Skin rights of burnt offerings are followed by grain offering distribution rules.
Specific grain offering allocations expand into equal distribution among priests.
The principle of one law echoes New Testament teaching on God of both Jews and Gentiles.
Priestly consumption of holy offerings foreshadows participation in Christ's altar.
General law of peace offerings is followed by specific thanksgiving requirements.
Unleavened requirements are expanded with inclusion of leavened bread.
Bread instructions transition into priestly portion allocation.
Heave offering portion is followed by consumption timing rules.
Thanksgiving eating is restricted to one day, while vow offerings extend longer.
Allowed eating window is followed by disposal instructions for leftovers.
Failure to properly dispose or consume leads to invalidation of the offering.
Proper handling of sacrificial meat echoes holiness requirements in burnt offerings.
Peace offering law continues themes established in earlier sacrificial regulations.
Instruction about unclean sacrificial meat is followed by penalties for eating while unclean.
The penalty is expanded to include multiple sources of uncleanness.
Uncleanness laws transition into dietary prohibition of fat.
General prohibition on fat is narrowed with exceptions for non-sacred use.
Permitted uses of fat are followed by prohibition on eating sacrificial fat.
Fat laws transition into broader prohibition on consuming blood.
The prohibition is reinforced with consequences for eating blood.
Transition from introduction to peace offering instructions.
Peace offering presentation is followed by wave offering procedure.
Wave offering leads to priestly allocation of portions.
Priestly portions are further specified to include the right shoulder.
Specific portion rules are expanded into a perpetual statute.
Statute is linked to priestly anointing and ordination context.
Priestly portion explanation is summarized as a perpetual command.
Final summary of sacrificial laws is followed by historical framing at Sinai.
God’s initiation of the ordination instructions is followed by the gathering of necessary people and materials.
Preparation of materials leads to assembling the congregation for the ordination ceremony.
The assembled congregation is addressed by Moses announcing divine command.
After announcement, the ritual purification of Aaron and his sons begins.
After washing, the priestly garments are placed upon Aaron.
Priestly washing parallels purification requirements in burnt offering rituals.
Aaronic priestly clothing foreshadows Christ as the great high priest.
After clothing and consecration begins, priestly garments are completed with breastplate.
Breastplate installation is followed by completion of head garments and crown.
After clothing the priest, Moses begins sanctification of tabernacle items.
Anointing of tabernacle is extended to altar and vessels.
After sanctifying sacred space, Aaron himself is anointed.
After high priest anointing, Aaron’s sons are clothed for service.
High priest sanctification foreshadows Christ as the ultimate holy high priest.
Anointing of tabernacle parallels earlier Exodus instructions for consecration of sacred space.
Sin offering begins consecration process with laying on of hands followed by blood application.
After purification of the altar, fat portions are burned as offering.
Sacrificial portions are offered to God while remaining body is removed outside camp.
After sin offering, burnt offering ram is presented for priestly consecration.
Burnt offering is followed by blood sprinkling on altar.
After blood ritual, ram is cut and burned in ordered sequence.
Final washing precedes complete burning of the ram.
The ram of consecration is followed by the blood application ritual.
Aaron’s consecration is extended to his sons using the same blood ritual.
Blood consecration is followed by selection of sacrificial portions.
Meat portions are accompanied by grain offerings in the consecration ritual.
Offerings are placed into the hands of priests for wave offering before the LORD.
Wave offering is followed by burning on the altar as consecration sacrifice.
Consecration offerings are followed by Moses receiving designated portions.
Moses’ portioning expands into full sanctification of Aaron and his sons through oil and blood.
Sanctification is followed by the consecration meal at the tabernacle entrance.
Meal consumption is followed by burning remaining sacred portions.
Completion of offerings leads to a seven-day consecration period of separation.
Moses summarizes the ordination process as commanded by the LORD.
The command is intensified with strict warning of death for disobedience.
Obedience to the command results in complete compliance by Aaron and his sons.
Moses calls Aaron and leaders, then instructs sacrificial preparation.
Priestly offerings expand into national offerings.
Israel brings offerings and assembles before the LORD.
Assembly is followed by instruction for atonement.
Command to make atonement leads to Aaron's first sacrifice.
Sin offering is followed by burnt offering ritual.
Burnt offering leads to communal sin offering.
Sin offering transitions into grain offering.
Offerings culminate in Aaron’s blessing of the people.
Blessing precedes divine manifestation of glory.
God’s glory is followed by fire consuming the offering.
Unauthorized offering directly results in divine judgment by fire.
After judgment, Moses interprets the event as a demonstration of God's holiness.
The theological explanation is followed by practical instructions to remove the bodies.
After removal, priests are instructed regarding mourning restrictions.
Mourning restrictions are followed by further holiness requirements regarding alcohol.
Purity requirements are followed by instructions on proper consumption of offerings.
Instruction about offerings leads to corrective inquiry about possible mishandling.
Moses' inquiry prompts Aaron's explanation regarding circumstances of the sin offering.
Both emphasize sanctity and correct worship before God.
Divine fire of judgment contrasts with earlier divine fire of acceptance.
General instruction is followed by specific dietary criteria for land animals.
Positive criteria are followed by exceptions limiting what is actually permitted.
Land animal laws are followed by aquatic animal dietary laws.
Clean fish criteria are followed by declaration of unclean aquatic life.
Aquatic laws are followed by classification of unclean birds.
Both land and bird classifications reflect the broader holiness distinction in diet.
Clean vs unclean classification system is applied across domains (land vs sea).
General dietary principle is summarized in explicit clean/unclean declarations.
The list of unclean birds continues from vultures and kites to ravens.
Additional unclean birds are sequentially identified.
The dietary prohibition list continues with more birds classified as unclean.
The sequence of prohibited birds proceeds through additional species.
The final set of unclean birds concludes the bird prohibition section.
The dietary laws expand from birds specifically named to broader classes of winged creeping creatures.
An exception is introduced to the general prohibition against flying creeping things.
The permitted category of leaping insects is expanded with specific examples.
Approved insects are contrasted with all remaining flying creeping things deemed abominable.
The dietary restrictions concerning unclean creatures are later addressed in Peter's vision regarding covenantal inclusion.
John the Baptist's consumption of locusts reflects the continuing recognition of permitted insects under Jewish dietary law.
Jesus' teaching concerning purity and defilement reframes ceremonial food distinctions.
The declaration of uncleanness is followed by required purification procedures.
Both passages command washing clothes after handling unclean carcasses.
The category of unclean beasts is expanded to include pawed animals.
Contact with pawed carcasses causes the need for ceremonial washing.
The list of unclean creeping creatures continues with additional species.
Both passages teach temporary ceremonial uncleanness through contact.
Ritual washing after impurity continues in later Mosaic purification laws.
Isaiah later condemns participation in practices involving unclean creatures.
The impurity from touching carcasses is expanded to include contamination of objects.
Once objects become unclean, washing procedures are prescribed for purification.
Some contaminated objects may be washed, but earthen vessels must be destroyed.
Both commands require destruction of contaminated porous items.
The passage distinguishes situations where impurity transfers from situations where it does not.
Dry seed remains clean, while wet seed becomes unclean through carcass contact.
Contact with or consumption of carcasses requires ceremonial washing.
Both passages teach that touching unclean things produces ceremonial impurity until evening.
Water purification rituals continue throughout Mosaic ceremonial law.
New Testament dietary and purity discussions revisit Mosaic distinctions concerning clean and unclean animals.
General prohibition of creeping things is expanded into specific classifications of unclean creatures.
Identification of unclean animals leads to warning against personal defilement.
Avoidance of defilement is intensified into a call to holiness reflecting God's nature.
Holiness command is grounded in redemption from Egypt and covenant identity.
Covenant holiness principle is summarized as the law of clean and unclean creatures.
Summary statement concludes with the purpose of distinguishing clean and unclean for Israel.
Holiness command is reframed as practical discernment in dietary practice.
After defining postpartum impurity, circumcision is commanded on the eighth day.
Circumcision is followed by the mother’s continued purification period.
The law contrasts male and female childbirth purification durations.
After purification time, offerings are required for restoration.
Offerings are completed by priestly atonement and declaration of cleansing.
Circumcision continues Abrahamic covenant practice established in Genesis 17.
Post-birth offerings continue into Second Temple practice seen in Luke 2.
